question: What’s an ideal timing sequence?
content:
Regardless of style (one handed or two handed), correct timing always follows this sequence:
1.	Controlled start
The first step(s) establish rhythm and balance.
The pushaway is smooth, slow, and connected to the feet, never rushed.
The ball stays in front of the body during the early steps.
2.	Pushaway finishes as the next step lands
The forward motion of the ball ends exactly as the next step plants on the floor.
At this moment, gravity takes over.
This is when the swing officially begins.
3.	Free swing begins (down and back)
The ball falls naturally into the swing.
No arm muscle is used to start or control the swing.
Swing direction stays aligned with the target.
4.	Feet increase tempo to match the swing
Once the ball is moving freely, the feet speed up naturally.
The body stays ahead of the ball.
This creates inertia and prevents early timing.
5.	Correct foul line relationship (the timing checkpoint)
The slide foot is flat and stable at the foul line.
At the same moment, the ball is still parallel (or very close) to the floor.
The body has stopped; the swing finishes afterward.
This final relationship is the only reliable indicator of correct timing.
